debt_collection.name: Debt Collection Notice
debt_collection.governing_law: Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA), Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) regulations
key_rights: Right to request debt validation within 30 days — collector must stop until they verify
Collectors cannot call before 8am or after 9pm
You can demand in writing that a collector stop contacting you
Collectors cannot threaten arrest, sue you in the wrong court, or use false statements
Right to file complaint with CFPB at ConsumerFinance.gov — free
Statute of limitations on debt varies by state: typically 3–6 years
common_violations: Threatening arrest for civil debt (illegal under FDCPA Section 807)
Calling outside permitted hours or calling repeatedly to harass
Not providing written verification of debt when requested
Misrepresenting the debt amount, collector identity, or legal status
Contacting third parties (employer, family) about the debt
debt_collection.forum: CFPB Complaint Portal or Federal District Court
debt_collection.template_response: Pursuant to the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (15 U.S.C. § 1692g), I am requesting written verification of the debt referenced in your notice. I dispute this debt and request that you cease all collection activity until verification is provided. I am aware of my rights under the FDCPA and will file a complaint with the CFPB if any violations occur.

eviction_notice.name: Eviction / Unlawful Detainer Notice
eviction_notice.governing_law: State landlord-tenant law (varies by state), local rent control ordinances
key_rights: 3-day notice to pay or quit (most states) before eviction filing
Right to a court hearing before being removed
Landlord cannot conduct self-help eviction (change locks, remove belongings)
Right to contest eviction in court within the stated period
Many states have tenant legal aid organizations — often free
common_violations: Changing locks or removing belongings without court order (illegal in all states)
Retaliatory eviction after tenant reports housing code violations
Insufficient notice period for the eviction type
Failure to return security deposit with itemized deductions
eviction_notice.forum: Local Housing Court or Small Claims Court
eviction_notice.template_response: I acknowledge receipt of your notice dated [DATE]. I dispute the stated grounds for eviction and intend to contest this matter in court. I am aware that self-help eviction is illegal in this state and that only a court can order my removal. I request all further communication in writing.

court_summons.name: Court Summons / Civil Complaint
court_summons.governing_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or State Rules of Civil Procedure
key_rights: 21 days to file a response (Answer) to a federal complaint; varies by state
Failure to respond results in default judgment against you
Right to legal representation; low-income individuals may qualify for free legal aid
Right to challenge jurisdiction if you were improperly served
common_violations: Improper service of process
Filing in a court without proper jurisdiction
Summons that does not include required information
court_summons.template_response: I am in receipt of the summons and complaint in Case No. [NUMBER]. I intend to respond within the time allowed by law. I am seeking legal counsel to represent me in this matter.

employment_termination.name: Termination / Separation Letter
employment_termination.governing_law: WARN Act (100+ employees), FLSA, FMLA, state Employment Standards Acts, EEOC anti-discrimination laws
key_rights: WARN Act requires 60-day notice for mass layoffs at companies with 100+ employees
Right to file EEOC complaint within 180 days if termination was discriminatory
Right to COBRA continuation health coverage for up to 18 months
Right to unemployment benefits — file with state unemployment office immediately
Final paycheck must be delivered by next regular payday in most states
common_violations: Termination based on protected characteristics (race, gender, age, disability, religion)
Retaliation for whistleblowing or filing a workplace complaint
WARN Act violation — insufficient notice for mass layoff
Failure to pay out accrued vacation in states that require it
employment_termination.forum: EEOC then Federal District Court, or State Labor Board
employment_termination.template_response: I acknowledge my termination as communicated on [DATE]. I request written confirmation of my final paycheck date, COBRA eligibility information, and any accrued but unused vacation payout. I reserve all rights to challenge this termination if it was in violation of applicable law, including anti-discrimination statutes.

consumer_notice.name: Consumer / Company Notice
consumer_notice.governing_law: FTC Act, state consumer protection laws, Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act
key_rights: Right to file complaint with FTC at ReportFraud.ftc.gov
Right to file complaint with State Attorney General
Small claims court for disputes typically up to $5,000–$15,000 (varies by state)
Written warranty must be honored under Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act
Right to chargeback through credit card company for goods/services not received
common_violations: Deceptive advertising or misleading product representations
Refusing to honor written warranty
Charging undisclosed fees
Bait-and-switch tactics
consumer_notice.forum: FTC, State Attorney General, or Small Claims Court
consumer_notice.template_response: I write to formally dispute [describe issue]. I purchased [product/service] on [date] for [amount]. The company has failed to [describe failure]. I request resolution within 14 days. If unresolved, I will file complaints with the FTC and the [State] Attorney General's Office and pursue remedies in small claims court.
